content:
# Judges Chapter 10 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_279.jpg, JDG_page_280.jpg

1. After Abimelech there arose to save Israel Tola the son of Puah, son of Dodo, a man of Issachar, and he lived at Shamir in the hill country of Ephraim.

2. And he judged Israel twenty-three years. Then he died and was buried at Shamir.

3. After him arose Jair the Gileadite, who judged Israel twenty-two years.

4. And he had thirty sons who rode on thirty donkeys, and they had thirty cities, called Havvoth-jair to this day, which are in the land of Gilead.

5. And Jair died and was buried in Kamon.

6. The people of Israel again did what was evil in the sight of the LORD and served the Baals and the Ashtaroth, the gods of Syria, the gods of Sidon, the gods of Moab, the gods of the Ammonites, and the gods of the Philistines. And they forsook the LORD and did not serve him.

7. So the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel, and he sold them into the hand of the Philistines and into the hand of the Ammonites,

8. and they crushed and oppressed the people of Israel that year. For eighteen years they oppressed all the people of Israel who were beyond the Jordan in the land of the Amorites, which is in Gilead.

9. And the Ammonites crossed the Jordan to fight also against Judah and against Benjamin and against the house of Ephraim, so that Israel was greatly distressed.

10. And the people of Israel cried out to the LORD, saying, "We have sinned against you, because we have forsaken our God and have served the Baals."

11. And the LORD said to the people of Israel, "Did I not save you from the Egyptians and from the Amorites, from the Ammonites and from the Philistines?

12. The Sidonians also, and the Amalekites and the Maonites oppressed you, and you cried out to me, and I saved you out of their hand.

13. Yet you have forsaken me and served other gods; therefore I will save you no more.

14. Go and cry out to the gods whom you have chosen; let them save you in the time of your distress."

15. And the people of Israel said to the LORD, "We have sinned; do to us whatever seems good to you. Only please deliver us this day."

16. So they put away the foreign gods from among them and served the LORD, and he became impatient over the misery of Israel.

17. Then the Ammonites were called to arms, and they encamped in Gilead. And the people of Israel came together, and they encamped at Mizpah.

18. And the people, the leaders of Gilead, said one to another, "Who is the man who will begin to fight against the Ammonites? He shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead."

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 10 has two movements: the brief "minor judge" notices for Tola and Jair (vv.1-5), and the most theologically sophisticated version of the Judges cycle (vv.6-18). The seven-fold catalog of foreign gods (v.6) signals total apostasy, and God's response breaks the pattern — for the first and only time in Judges, God says "I will save you no more" (v.13). Israel's response is also unprecedented: they don't just cry out but confess, surrender to God's judgment, and put away their idols (v.15-16). The result is the most striking anthropomorphism in Judges: God's soul "was shortened" over Israel's misery (v.16).

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.7 — "sold them" vs. "turned them over"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he sold them into the hand of" | vayyimkerem b'yad |
| LOGOS | "he sold them into the hand of" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "he turned them over to" | Avoids commercial metaphor |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Consistent with resolutions in 2:14, 3:8, 4:2. The "selling" metaphor (makar) is a recurring theological motif in Judges.

**Status:** Minor. Recurring — resolved consistently.

---

### v.16 — "he became impatient over the misery of Israel" vs. "his soul was shortened" vs. "he was grieved by their misery"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his soul was shortened over the misery of Yisrael" | vatiqtsar nafsho — literal |
| LOGOS | "he became impatient over the misery of Israel" | Interprets qatsar nefesh as impatience |
| PNEUMA | "he was grieved by their misery" | Interprets as emotional grief |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS with note. The Hebrew vatiqtsar nafsho b'amal Yisrael is a rich idiom. qatsar nefesh literally means "his soul/self was shortened" — it expresses the inability to endure something any longer. LOGOS's "became impatient" captures the sense of reaching a limit. PNEUMA's "grieved" is too passive — the idiom implies active restlessness, not mere sorrow. EMET's "his soul was shortened" is vivid but opaque in English. The consensus adds this note: the idiom conveys that God could no longer bear to watch Israel suffer, despite his own declaration that he would not save them.

**Status:** Major. Key theological anthropomorphism — the pivot from divine refusal to divine mercy.

---

### v.6 — "Ashtaroth" vs. "Ashtoreths" vs. "images of Baal"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Ashtarot" | Hebrew plural, transliterated |
| LOGOS | "the Ashtaroth" | Standard English transliteration |
| PNEUMA | "the Baals and the Ashtoreths, and the gods of..." | Uses anglicized plural |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Ashtaroth" is the standard English transliteration of the Hebrew plural ashtarot (the plural of Ashtoreth, the Canaanite goddess). PNEUMA's "Ashtoreths" creates a double-plural ("Ashtoreth" + English "-s") which is grammatically redundant since "Ashtaroth" already is the plural.

**Status:** Minor. Transliteration convention.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Recurring "sold" metaphor | 1 | Consistent with prior chapters |
| Key anthropomorphism | 1 | "Soul shortened" — patience, grief, or both? (v.16) |
| Minor transliteration | 1 | Ashtaroth plural form (v.6) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 10's most significant disagreement is v.16 — the anthropomorphism of God's "shortened soul." This is a theological crux: God declared he would not save Israel (v.14), yet his own inner life compels him to act. The translation of this idiom determines whether God is portrayed as "impatient" (LOGOS — reaching a limit), "grieved" (PNEUMA — emotionally moved), or something more visceral (EMET — "shortened soul"). All three capture a facet of the Hebrew; LOGOS is closest to the idiom's core meaning.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
